{{Short description|Idea that everything in the universe will converge to a final point of unification}}
{{Other uses}}
The '''Omega Point''' is a supposed future when everything in the universe spirals toward a final point of unification.<ref name="newsweek">{{Cite news |title=Could artificial intelligence kill us off? |work=[[Newsweek]] |url=http://europe.newsweek.com/could-artificial-intelligence-kill-us-off-329208 |access-date=21 September 2016}}</ref> The term was invented by the French [[Society of Jesus|Jesuit]] Catholic [[Catholic priesthood|priest]] [[Pierre Teilhard de Chardin]] (1881–1955).<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Castillo |first=Mauricio |date=March 2012 |title=The Omega Point and Beyond: The Singularity Event |url=http://www.ajnr.org/content/33/3/393.full.pdf |journal=American Journal of Neuroradiology |volume=33 |issue=3 |pages=393–5 |doi=10.3174/ajnr.A2664 |pmc=7966419 |pmid=21903920 |access-date=6 June 2015 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Teilhard argued that the Omega Point resembles the Christian [[Logos]], namely [[Christ the Logos|Christ]], who draws all things into himself, who in the words of the [[Nicene Creed]], is "God from God", "Light from Light", "True God from true God", and "through him all things were made".<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hickey |first=Michael |title=Get to the End: A Catholic's View of the End Times |date=2016-03-07 |publisher=UPA |isbn=9780761867333 |location=London |pages=104}}</ref> In the [[Book of Revelation]], Christ describes himself thrice as "[[the Alpha and the Omega]], the beginning and the end". The idea of the Omega Point is developed in later writings, such as those of John David Garcia (1971), [[Paolo Soleri]] (1981), [[Frank Tipler]] (1994), and [[David Deutsch]] (1997).<ref name="singularity">{{Cite journal |last=Steinhart |first=Eric |year=2008 |title=Teilhard de Chardin and Transhumanism |url=http://jetpress.org/v20/steinhart.htm |journal=Journal of Evolution and Technology |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=1–22 |issn=1541-0099 |access-date=2015-06-03}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Green |first=Ronald |year=2012 |title=Challenging Transhumanism's Values |journal=Hastings Center Report |volume=43 |issue=4 |pages=45–47 |doi=10.1002/hast.195}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Lilley |first=Stephen |title=Transhumanism and Society |chapter=Transcend or Transgress? |work=Hastings Center Report |year=2013 |isbn=978-94-007-4980-1 |series=SpringerBriefs in Philosophy |pages=13–24 |doi=10.1007/978-94-007-4981-8_2}}</ref>

==Pierre Teilhard de Chardin's theory==

[[File:TeilhardP 1947.jpg|thumb|Pierre Teilhard de Chardin, 1947]]

===Etymology===

[[Teilhard de Chardin]] was a [[Paleontology|paleontologist]] and Roman Catholic priest in the Jesuit order. In France in the 1920s, he began incorporating his theories of the universe into lectures that placed [[Catholicism]] and [[evolution]] in the same conversation. Because of these lectures, he was suspected by the [[Holy Office]] of denying the doctrine of [[original sin]]. This caused Teilhard to be exiled to China and banned from publication by Church authorities.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Bryant |first=M. Darrol |title=Out of Galilee: Christian Thought as a Great Conversation |date=2018-04-20 |publisher=Wipf and Stock Publishers |isbn=9781532638497 |location=Eugene, OR |pages=300}}</ref> It was not until one year after his death in 1955 that his writings were published for the world to read. His works were also supported by the writings of a group of Catholic thinkers, which includes [[Pope Benedict XVI]].<ref name=":0" /> His book, ''[[The Phenomenon of Man]]'', has been dissected by astrophysicists and cosmologists to be a theological or philosophical [[theory]] that cannot be scientifically proven. Teilhard, who was not a [[cosmologist]], opens his books with the statement: 
{{blockquote|... if this book is to be properly understood, it must be read not as a work on metaphysics, still less as a sort of theological essay, but purely and simply as a scientific treatise.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Teilhard de Chardin |first=Pierre |url=https://archive.org/details/phenomenonman00char |title=The Phenomenon of Man |date=2008 |publisher=Harper Perennial Modern Thought |isbn=978-0061632655 |location=New York |page=[https://archive.org/details/phenomenonman00char/page/n15 29] |translator-last=Wall |translator-first=Bernard |author-link=Pierre Teilhard de Chardin |url-access=limited}}</ref>}}

===Evolution===

According to Teilhard, [[evolution]] does not end with mankind and Earth's [[biosphere]] evolved before humans existed. He described evolution as a progression that begins with inanimate matter to a future state of Divine consciousness through Earth's "hominization".<ref>{{Cite book |last=Livingstone |first=David |title=Black Terror White Soldiers: Islam, Fascism & the New Age |date=2013 |publisher=Sabilillah Publications |isbn=9781481226509 |pages=451}}</ref> He also maintained that one-cell [[organisms]] develop into metazoans or animals, but some of the members of this classification develop organisms with complex [[nervous systems]]. This group has the capability to acquire [[intelligence]]. When ''[[Homo sapiens]]'' inhabited Earth through evolution, a [[noosphere]], the cognitive layer of existence, was created. As evolution continues, the noosphere gains coherence. Teilhard explained that this noosphere can be moved toward or constructed to be the Omega Point or the final evolutionary stage with the help of science.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Feist |first1=Richard |title=Religion and the Challenges of Science |last2=Sweet |first2=William |date=2013 |publisher=Ashgate Publishing, Ltd. |isbn=9781409477624 |location=Burlington, VT |pages=66}}</ref> Teilhard refers to this process as "planetization". Eventually, the noosphere gains total dominance over the biosphere and reaches a point of complete independence from tangential energy forming a metaphysical being, coined the Omega Point.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tipler |first=Frank J. |url=https://archive.org/details/physicsimmortali00tipl_155 |title=The physics of immortality: modern cosmology, God, and the resurrection of the dead |date=1994 |publisher=Anchor Books |isbn=978-0385467995 |edition=1st Anchor Books |location=New York |page=[https://archive.org/details/physicsimmortali00tipl_155/page/n141 113] |url-access=limited}}</ref>

===Energy===

[[Energeia|Energy]] exists in two basic modes:
# "Tangential Energy": energy that can be [[Energy|measured by physics]].
# "Radial Energy": [[spiritual energy]] which accumulates into a higher state as time progresses.

Teilhard defines Radial Energy as becoming more concentrated and available as it is a critical element in man's evolution. The theory applies to all forms of matter, concluding that everything with existence has some sort of life. In regard to Teilhard's ''The Phenomenon of Man'', [[Peter Medawar]] wrote, "Teilhard's radial, spiritual, or psychic energy may be equated to 'information' or 'information content' in the sense that has been made reasonably precise by communication engineers."<ref>{{Cite web |last=Medawar |first=Sir Peter |title=The Phenomenon of Man |url=http://bactra.org/Medawar/phenomenon-of-man.html |access-date=16 February 2018 |website=bactra.org}}</ref>

===Formal properties===

Teilhard's theory is maintained by four formal properties:
# Humans will escape the [[heat death of the universe]]. Current scientific understandings imply that intelligence cannot survive heat death.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tipler |first=Frank J. |url=https://archive.org/details/physicsimmortali00tipl_155 |title=The physics of immortality : modern cosmology, God, and the resurrection of the dead |date=1994 |publisher=Anchor Books |isbn=978-0385467995 |edition=1st Anchor Books |location=New York |page=[https://archive.org/details/physicsimmortali00tipl_155/page/n139 111] |url-access=limited}}</ref> He theorizes that since radial energy is non-compliant with entropy, it escapes the collapses of forces at world's end.
# The Omega Point does not exist within the timeline of the universe, it occurs at the exact edge of the end of time. From that point, all sequences of existence are sucked into its being.
# The Omega Point can be understood as a volume shaped like a cone in which each section taken from the base to its summit decreases until it diminishes into a final point.
# The volume described in the Third Property must be understood as an entity with finite boundaries. Teilhard explains: 
{{blockquote|...&nbsp;what would have become of humanity, if, by some remote chance, it had been free to spread indefinitely on an unlimited surface, that is to say, left only to the devices of its internal affinities? Something unimaginable.&nbsp;... Perhaps even nothing at all, when we think of the extreme importance of the role played in its development by the forces of compression.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Teilhard de Chardin|first=Pierre |url=https://archive.org/details/phenomenonman00char |title=The phenomenon of man |date=2008 |publisher=Harper Perennial Modern Thought |isbn=978-0061632655 |edition=1st |location=New York |page=[https://archive.org/details/phenomenonman00char/page/n120 239] |translator-first=Bernard |translator-last=Wall|url-access=limited}}</ref>}}

===Forces of compression===

Teilhard calls the contributing universal energy that generates the Omega Point "forces of compression". Unlike the scientific definition, which incorporates [[gravity]] and [[mass]], Teilhard's forces of compression sources from communication and contact between human beings. This value is limitless and directly correlated with entropy. It suggests that as humans continue to interact, [[consciousness]] evolves and grows. For the theory to occur, humans must also be bound to the finite earth. The creation of this boundary forces the world's convergence upon itself which he theorizes to result in time ending in communion with the Omega Point-God. This portion of Teilhard's thinking shows his lack of expectation for humans to engage in space travel and transcend past the borders of the [[planet]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Tipler |first=Frank J. |url=https://archive.org/details/physicsimmortali00tipl_155 |title=The physics of immortality : modern cosmology, God, and the resurrection of the dead |date=1994 |publisher=Anchor Books |isbn=978-0385467995 |edition=1st Anchor Books |location=New York |page=[https://archive.org/details/physicsimmortali00tipl_155/page/n143 115] |url-access=limited}}</ref>

== The Omega Point cosmology ==
{{Main|Frank J. Tipler#The Omega Point cosmology}}

[[File:Omega-Point-Multiverse.png|thumb|Frank J. Tipler's multiverse theory]]

Mathematical physicist [[Frank J. Tipler|Frank Tipler]] generalizes<ref>Tipler, Frank J. "The omega point as eschaton: Answers to Pannenberg's questions for scientists." [[Zygon (journal)]] 24.2 (1989): 217–253. "Needless to say, the terminology is Teilhard de Chardin's..."</ref> Teilhard's term ''Omega Point'' to describe what he maintains is the [[ultimate fate of the universe]] required by the [[laws of physics]]: roughly, Tipler argues that quantum mechanics is inconsistent unless the future of every point in spacetime contains an intelligent observer to collapse the wavefunction and that the only way for this to happen is if the Universe is closed (that is, it will collapse to a single point) and yet contains observers with a "God-like" ability to perform an unbounded series of observations in finite time.<ref name="ellis1994">{{Cite journal |last=Ellis |first=George Francis Rayner |author-link=George Francis Rayner Ellis |date=1994 |title=Piety in the Sky |url=http://impleri.net/files/ellis.pdf |journal=[[Nature (journal)|Nature]] |volume=371 |issue=6493 |page=115 |bibcode=1994Natur.371..115E |doi=10.1038/371115a0 |s2cid=36282720 |quote=It is a masterpiece of pseudoscience}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Krauss |first=Lawrence |date=May 2007 |title=More dangerous than nonsense |pages=53 |journal=[[New Scientist]] |volume=194 |issue=2603 |doi=10.1016/S0262-4079(07)61199-3 |quote=I am tempted to describe Tipler's new book as nonsense—but that would be unfair to the concept of nonsense.}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |date=2008 |title=The Strange Case of Frank Jennings Tipler |work=[[Skeptical Inquirer]] |url=http://www.csicop.org/si/show/the_strange_case_of_frank_jennings_tipler |access-date=21 September 2016 |quote=I began to wonder if the book could be a subtle, hilarious hoax. Sadly, it is not.}}</ref>

==Theological controversy==
{{Essay-like|date=August 2022}}
Pierre Teilhard de Chardin's life (1881–1955) falls directly in between the [[First Vatican Council]] (1869) and the [[Second Vatican Council]] (1965). His time came shortly after [[Charles Darwin]]'s 1859 book, ''[[On the Origin of Species]]'', a time when the intersection between the claims of scientific theories and the claims of traditional theological teachings became an enormous focus of the Vatican's agenda.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Trennert-Helwig |first=Mathias |date=March 1995 |title=The Church as the Axis of Convergence in Teilhard's Theology and Life |journal=Zygon |volume=30 |pages=73–89 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9744.1995.tb00052.x}}</ref>

[[Pope Pius XII]] stated his concern on the [[theory of evolution]], albeit without condemning it:
{{blockquote|If such a doctrine were to be spread, what will become of the unchangeable Catholic dogmas, what of the unity and the stability of the Creed?<ref>{{Cite journal |date=19 September 1946 |title=6 "Si talis opinio amplectanda esse videatur, quidfiet de numquam immutandis catholias dogmatibus, quid de fidei unitale et stabuliat |journal=L'Osservatore Romano}}</ref>}}

Teilhard's theory was a personal attempt in creating a new Christianity in which science and theology coexist{{Citation needed|reason=Cite writings from de Chardin supporting this assertion rather than merely an attempt to include scientific discoveries into existing Catholic theology.|date=October 2018}}. The outcome was that his theory of the Omega Point was not perfectly scientific as examined by physicists, and not perfectly Christian either. By 1962, [[Society of Jesus|The Society of Jesus]] had strayed from Spanish Jesuit Priest [[Francisco Suarez]]'s philosophies on Man in favor of "Teilhardian evolutionary cosmogenesis". Teilhard's Christ is the "[[Cosmic Christ]]" or the "Omega" of revelation. He is an emanation of God which is made of matter and experienced the nature of evolution by being born into this world and dying. His resurrection from the dead was not to heaven, but to the noosphere, the area of convergence of all spirituality and spiritual beings, where Christ will be waiting at the end of time. When the earth reaches its Omega Point, everything that exists will become one with divinity.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Teilhard de Chardin |first=Pierre |url=https://archive.org/details/sciencechrist0000teil |title=Science and Christ |date=1 January 1968 |publisher=Collins |url-access=registration}}</ref>

Teilhard reaffirms the role of the Church in the following letter to Auguste Valensin. It is important to note that he defines evolution as a scientific phenomenon set in motion by God – that science and the divine are interconnected and acting through one another:
{{blockquote|I believe in the Church, [[mediatrix]] between God and the world[.] ... The Church, the reflectively christified portion of the world, the Church, the principal focus of inter-human affinities through super-charity, the Church, the central axis of universal convergence and the precise point of contact between the universe and Omega Point. ... The Catholic Church, however, must not simply seek to affirm its primacy and authority but quite simply to present the world with the Universal Christ, Christ in human-cosmic dimension, as the animator of evolution.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Teilhard de Chardin |first=Pierre |date=1948 |title=My Fundamental Vision |volume=XI |pages=191–192}}</ref>}}

==Related concepts==

=== Accelerating expansion of the universe ===
{{Main|Accelerating expansion of the universe}}
In 1998, a value measured from observations of [[Type Ia supernova]]e seemed to indicate that what was once assumed to be temporary cosmological expansion was actually accelerating.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Palmer |first=Jason |date=4 October 2011 |title=Nobel physics prize honours accelerating Universe find |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/science-environment-15165371}}</ref> The apparent acceleration has caused many to dismiss Tipler's Omega Point out of hand since the necessity of a final big crunch singularity is key to the Omega Point's workability. However, Tipler himself believes that the Omega Point is still workable and has explained on multiple occasions why a big crunch/ final singularity is still required under many current universal models.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Prisco |first=Giulio |date=26 September 2012 |title=Interview with Frank J. Tipler (Nov. 2002) |url=http://turingchurch.com/2012/09/26/interview-with-frank-j-tipler-nov-2002/ |website=Turing Church}}</ref><ref>Archived at [https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211211/kMkp1kZN5n4 Ghostarchive]{{cbignore}} and the [https://web.archive.org/web/20181104193422/https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kMkp1kZN5n4&gl=US&hl=en Wayback Machine]{{cbignore}}: {{Cite AV media |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kMkp1kZN5n4&t=26s |title=White Gardenia interview with Frank Tipler |date=December 2015}}{{cbignore}}</ref>

===Technological singularity===
The [[technological singularity]] is the hypothetical advent of [[artificial general intelligence]] theoretically capable of recursive self-improvement, resulting in a runaway effect to an [[intelligence explosion]].<ref>Chalmers, David. "The singularity: A philosophical analysis." Journal of Consciousness Studies 17.9-10 (2010): 7-65.</ref> Eric Steinhart, a proponent of "[[Christian transhumanism]]", argues there is a significant overlap of ideas between the secular singularity and [[Pierre Teilhard de Chardin|Teilhard]]'s religious Omega Point.<ref name=singularity /> Steinhart quotes [[Ray Kurzweil]], one of the most prominent singularitarians, who stated that "evolution moves inexorably toward our conception of God, albeit never reaching this ideal."<ref name=singularity /><ref>{{Cite book |last=Kurzweil |first=Ray |title=The Singularity is Near |title-link=The Singularity is Near |publisher=Viking Books |year=2005 |isbn=978-0-670-03384-3 |location=New York}}, pg 476; see also 375, 389-390</ref>  Like Kurzweil, [[Pierre Teilhard de Chardin|Teilhard]] predicts a period of rapid technological change that results in a merger of humanity and technology. He believes that this marks the birth of the [[noosphere]] and the emergence of the "spirit of the Earth", but the [[Pierre Teilhard de Chardin|Teilhardian Singularity]] comes later. Unlike Kurzweil, [[Pierre Teilhard de Chardin|Teilhard]]'s singularity is marked by the evolution of human intelligence reaching a critical point in which humans ascend from "transhuman" to "posthuman". He identifies this with the Christian [[Second Coming|"parousia"]].<ref name="singularity" />

==In popular culture==
{{related|[[Apollo 440#Omega Point]]}}
The Spanish painter [[Salvador Dalí]] was fascinated by Teilhard de Chardin and the Omega Point theory. His 1959 painting ''[[The Ecumenical Council (painting)|The Ecumenical Council]]'' is said to represent the "interconnectedness" of the Omega Point.<ref>{{Cite web |title=VCE Art: The Ecumenical Council |url=http://www.ngv.vic.gov.au/dali/salvador/resources/vce_FINAL.pdf |publisher=National Gallery of Victoria Educational Resource}}</ref> ''[[Point Omega]]'' by [[Don DeLillo]] takes its name from the theory and involves a character who is studying Teilhard de Chardin.<ref>{{Cite book |last=DeLillo |first=Don |title=Point Omega |publisher=Scribner |year=2010}}</ref> [[Flannery O'Connor]]'s acclaimed collection of short stories taps the Omega Point theory in its title, ''[[Everything That Rises Must Converge]]'', and science fiction writer [[Frederik Pohl]] references Frank Tipler and the Omega Point in his 1998 short story "The Siege of Eternity".<ref>{{Cite book |last=Pohl |first=Frederik |title=The Siege of Eternity |publisher=Tor Science Fiction |year=1998 |isbn=978-0812577662}}</ref> Scottish writer / counterculture figure [[Grant Morrison]] has used the Omega Point as a plot line in several of his Justice League of America and Batman stories.<ref>Morrison, Grant. Sample page from ''[https://img.purch.com/o/aHR0cDovL3d3dy5uZXdzYXJhbWEuY29tL2ltYWdlcy9pLzAwMC8wNTUvNDczL29yaWdpbmFsL2JtcmJ3XzJfZHlsdXgtNC01LWNvcHkuanBn Batman: The Return of Bruce Wayne]'' #2 (June 2010). DC Comics.</ref><ref>Morrison, Grant. Sample page from ''JLA'' Volume 3: "[https://images-na.ssl-images-amazon.com/images/S/cmx-images-prod/Item/4697/Previews/da389cc1263a70a33f4e4dbf75b9e303._SX1280_QL80_TTD_.jpg The Rock of Ages]".</ref><ref>Morrison, Grant. Sample page from ''[https://images-na.ssl-images-amazon.com/images/S/cmx-images-prod/Item/18826/Previews/9b60a1eb22dfc4ff6a8b4fe745b232ab._SX1280_QL80_TTD_.jpg Batman: The Return of Bruce Wayne]'' #6 (November 2010). DC Comics.</ref> 
Arthur C. Clarke and Stephen Baxter's ''[[The Light of Other Days]]'' references Teilhard de Chardin and includes a brief explanation of the Omega Point.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Clarke |first=Arthur C. |title=The Light of Other Days |publisher=Tom Doherty Associates |year=2001 |isbn=978-0-812-57640-5 |page=331}}</ref> Italian writer [[Valerio Evangelisti]] has used the Omega Point as main theme of his ''[[Il Fantasma di Eymerich]]'' novel.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Evangelisti |first=Valerio |title=Il Fantasma di Eymerich |publisher=Mondadori |year=2018 |isbn=978-8804702245}}</ref> In William Peter Blatty's novel ''[[The Exorcist (novel)|The Exorcist]],'' the character of Father Merrin references Omega Point. In 2021, Dutch symphonic metal band [[Epica (band)|Epica]] released their eighth studio album, [[Omega (Epica album)|Omega]], which features concepts related to the Omega Point theory. Epica's guitarist and vocalist, [[Mark Jansen]] specifically referenced Teilhard's theory when describing the album's concept.<ref>Jansen, Mark, [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MTPbOx9cMqo&t=189s Epica's VLOG from the Omega recording sessions].</ref>

==See also==
{{div col|colwidth=30em}}
Related concepts
* [[Apocatastasis]]
* [[Artificial life]]
* [[Eschatology]]
* [[Great chain of being]]
* [[Metasystem transition]]
* [[Timewave zero]]
* [[Noogenesis]]
* [[Posthuman God]]
* [[Superintelligence]]
* [[Supermind (integral yoga)]]
* [[Technological singularity]]
* [[Teleology]]
* [[Transhumanism]]
Related people
* [[Elisabet Sahtouris]]
* [[Nikolai Fyodorovich Fyodorov]]
* [[Vladimir Vernadsky]]
* [[Wolfhart Pannenberg]]
{{div col end}}

==References==
{{Reflist|30em}}

==External links==
* [http://www.idsia.ch/~juergen/computerhistory.html Computer history's stride towards an expected Omega Point] by [[Jürgen Schmidhuber]], from "The New AI: General & Sound & Relevant for Physics", In B. Goertzel and C. Pennachin, eds.: ''Artificial General Intelligence'', p. 175-198, 2006.
* [http://www.math.tulane.edu/~tipler/summary.html Essays by Tipler on the Omega Point]
* [http://www.humanevol.com Human Evolution Research Institute]
* [http://noosphere.princeton.edu/ Princeton Noosphere project] cites Teilhard de Chardin
* [http://www.kheper.net/topics/Teilhard/Teilhard-evolution.htm Teilhard de Chardin on evolution]

{{DEFAULTSORT:Omega Point}}
[[Category:Christian eschatology]]
[[Category:Concepts in metaphysics]]
[[Category:Mysticism]]
[[Category:Panentheism]]
[[Category:Pantheism]]
[[Category:Philosophy of religion]]
[[Category:Philosophy of science]]
[[Category:Philosophy of technology]]
[[Category:Religion and science]]
[[Category:Singularitarianism]]
[[Category:Nikolai Fedorov]]

[[he:פייר טיילר דה שרדן#רעיונותיו]]
[[Category:Pages]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
